https://doi.org/10.1038/s41580-023-00599-7

Heterochromatin definition and function Oliver Bell, Adam Burton, Caroline Dean, Susan M. Gasser & Maria-Elena Torres-Padilla Heterochromatin is a key characteristic of eukaryotic genomes. Since its cytological description nearly 100 years ago, our understanding of heterochromatin features and functions, including transcription repression and genome stability, have continuously evolved. In this Viewpoint article, experts provide their current opinions on hetero chromatin definition, types and functional mechanisms. How would you best define ‘heterochromatin’, and on what basis? Caroline Dean: Up until 20 years ago, the term heterochromatin referred to the visually distinct chromosomal regions one could see down a microscope. These days it is defined as chromatin marked by silencing modifications — DNA methylation and specific histone methylations that provide a feedback mechanism ensuring inheritance and maintenance of gene repression through DNA replication, even when the initial repressors are absent. Susan Gasser: Heterochromatin was classically defined as dark-staining chromatin, which contained a higher local concentration of nucleic acid and protein. The more modern definition is chromatin that is transcriptionally inactive and relatively compact or inaccessible to RNA polymerases. In most organisms, heterochromatin carries either histone H3 Lys9 dimethylation (H3K9me2) or H3K9me3 and/or Polycombmediated H3K27me3. Factors that recognize these modifications are generally enriched in heterochromatin domains, which cluster in the nucleus, both in cis and in trans. The ‘writers’ (enzymes that modify designated amino acids) and the ‘readers’ of these modifications tend to stay associated with heterochromatin to ensure maintenance of the heterochromatic state through replication. Certain, but not all, transcription factors are excluded by the heterochromatic chromatin state. Maria-Elena Torres-Padilla & Adam Burton: The best description of heterochromatin

encompasses three words: nucleation, spreading and maintenance. To our knowledge, there is no other type of chromatin or genomic region that combines these three features. Nucleation, spreading and maintenance incorporate our understanding of the molecular mechanisms — and behaviour — of heterochromatin that has emerged in the past three decades, but they also remain faithful to the original description of heterochromatin by Emil Heitz in 1928 (ref. 1), especially concerning the maintenance concept. Heitz studied chromosomal spreads from a moss, and later from many other plant species, and consistently identified regions of the chromosomes that were heavily stained and remained condensed throughout the cell cycle, which he referred to as heterochromatin. The term heterochromatin is useful as a contrast to euchromatin, and means regions of the genome lacking robust transcriptional activity. Furthermore, as recognized by Heitz, an additional distinction between ‘facultative’ and ‘constitutive’ heterochromatin must be drawn, as they are markedly different in terms of function and molecular characteristics (see below). Beyond the initial cytological definition, the term heterochromatin has further acquired additional descriptors ranging across functional, genetic and molecular levels. At the functional level, heterochromatin is mostly associated with transcription repression, replication late in the S phase, preservation of accurate chromosomal segregation and suppression of transposition. However, there are periods of time, either during development of multicellular organisms or during defined processes, when these functional features do not apply. For example, transcription occurs in classical heterochromatin regions in early development of mammals, and it is in fact required to nucleate heterochromatin in fission yeast. At the genetic level, across species, heterochromatin is enriched in repetitive sequences, including satellites and transposons, and is generally gene poor, although these characteristics apply more to constitutive than to facultative heterochromatin. Heterochromatin is generally localized around the peripheries of the nucleus and nucleolus and is primarily constrained to the genomic B compartment. Molecularly, relative to euchromatin, hetero chromatin has hypoacetylated histones,

Check for updates

is enriched in DNA methylation and distinct histone post-translational modifications (PTMs) and is associated with writer and reader protein complexes, which differ between constitutive and facultative heterochromatin. A crucial mark associated with constitutive heterochromatin in most — but not all — eukaryotes is H3K9me3, which is at the core of a functional cascade that allows the nucleation, spreading and maintenance of constitutive heterochromatin. Similarly, H3K27me3 can be viewed as the equivalent core for facultative heterochromatin. Although this general classification holds true, it must be emphasized that these features are not always matched, making a common definition of heterochromatin regions across time and space difficult. We suggest that hetero chromatin can be defined as regions of the eukaryotic genome that are able to nucleate, spread and maintain a mostly silent state and that possess distinctive molecular and genetic features. Oliver Bell: Heterochromatin can be broadly defined as chromosomal regions containing repetitive DNA sequences and tissuespecific gene regulatory elements that remain relatively inactive and inaccessible for DNAtemplated processes such as transcription, replication and repair compared to euchromatin regions. At the molecular level, genomewide profiling techniques such as chromatin immunoprecipitation coupled with deep sequencing (ChIP–seq) have revealed the existence of different heterochromatin subtypes based on chromosomal domains with distinct combinations of histone PTMs. Prominent examples are heterochromatin domains marked by methylation of either H3K9 or H3K27. These PTMs are recognized by specific readers, which in turn mediate recruitment of repressive effector proteins such as chromatin remodellers, structural proteins and the writers that add the PTMs to histones. Such positive feedback mechanisms between PTM readers and writers promote propagation of transcription-repressive chromatin domains, a characteristic that seems to be unique to heterochromatin and that I find most fascinating. In addition to enabling the spreading of PTM domains in cis from sequence-specific

[Page 2]

nucleation sites, this coupling of reader–writer activities facilitates epigenetic inheritance of heterochromatin through DNA replication and cell division, thereby allowing long-term maintenance of gene repression independently of the initial stimulus. Traditionally, heterochromatin has been classified as either constitutive or facultative — is this classification still valid in your opinion? Is there a more suitable classification? Should these two forms still be primarily framed as H3K9me-type heterochromatin and H3K27me3-type heterochromatin? C.D. I think the classification of heterochromatin into constitutive and facultative is less helpful these days. Perhaps heterochromatin should be classified according to the silencing modification that confers its epigenetic inheritance. For example, heterochromatin may be classified as a DNA-methylation, H3K9-methylation heterochromatin type or as an H3K27me3 heterochromatin type. There are subclasses of each type: for example, DNA methylation can be RNA-mediated H3K9me2-associated CHH DNA methylation or CG symmetric DNA methylation. S.G. The term constitutive heterochromatin means that a given domain remains compact and repressed in all cell types and at all stages of development, whereas facultative heterochromatin is repressed as a function of differentiation or cell type. This designation is still valid, as constitutive heterochromatin is heavily enriched in repetitive sequences (microsatellite and minisatellite repeats, centromeric and telomeric repeats) and many types of transposons (DNA and RNA transposons, with and without long terminal repeats), which remain repressed in all — or nearly all — cell types of an organism. By contrast, facultative heterochromatin usually coincides with genes that are expressed in a tissue-specific manner and are stably repressed in the cells of other tissues, thereby keeping ‘inappropriate’ genes silent. Polycomb-marked (H3K27me3) genes can have the unique characteristic of being held in a ‘poised’ but silent state in pluripotent stem cells, which can be switched during development to either a stably active or stably repressed state. It is debatable whether these gene domains represent real heterochromatin, as they often generate low levels of unstable transcripts, which keep the domain poised. It is not true that facultative heterochromatin always bears the Polycomb modification

The contributors Oliver Bell co-developed during his postdoctoral training with Dr Gerald R. Crabtree an innovative technology to artificially induce heterochromatin in cells and determine the properties of its spreading and heritability. In his own lab, he investigates the mechanisms of epigenetic gene silencing and the role of heterochromatin in regulation of cell identity. Adam Burton is Deputy Director of the Institute of Epigenetics and Stem Cells at the Helmholtz Centre Munich. He has been working with Maria-Elena Torres-Padilla on the establishment and function of heterochromatin during early mammalian development for the past 12 years. Caroline Dean investigates how plants use seasonal signals to judge when to flower, which has led to the study of conserved mechanisms of epigenetic regulation — specifically of Polycomb-mediated silencing. Her group studies how temperature

(H3K27me3). In fact, many genes are tissuespecifically repressed by heterochromatin carrying H3K9me2 or H3K9me3, and this repression can be reversed during development. Conversely, the silencing of some repeats depends on the Polycomb system. Thus, the functional definition stated above is more relevant and more accurate than one based on the specific histone H3 modification. M.E.T.P. & A.B. Yes, this classification still holds for most complex eukaryotes — but again, perhaps not for all. There are clear distinctions between the functions attributed to the two types of heterochromatin described above, whereby some are more associated with constitutive heterochromatin than with facultative heterochromatin, with the exception of transcriptional repression, which is typically common to both. Constitutive and facultative heterochromatin are distinct both molecularly and functionally. The distinction has only been reinforced in recent years. Nevertheless, in our view the most fitting distinction still harks back to the work of Heitz, who noted that facultative heterochromatin is cell-type-dependent whereas constitutive heterochromatin is celltype-invariant in a given species or organism1. Facultative heterochromatin has also emerged as a regulator of gene dosage — for example, in imprinting and X chromosome inactivation. Constitutive and facultative heterochromatin are often defined either cytologically or molecularly by the presence of molecular machineries and histone PTMs, notably H3K9me3 and HP1 proteins in constitutive heterochromatin and H3K27me3 and Polycomb repressive complex 1 (PRC1) and PRC2 in facultative heterochromatin. However, many other modifications are enriched in one or the

affects transcription and epigenetic dynamics and how these processes change as plants adapt to new conditions. Susan Gasser is a Swiss molecular biologist who has studied heterochromatin in yeast and in C. elegans since 1986. She had led a research group on chromatin organization and replication stress in Lausanne, in Geneva, and finally in Basel, and from 2004 to 2019, served as Director of the Friedrich Miescher Institute for Biomedical Research. She is now Director of the ISREC Foundation, Lausanne. Maria-Elena Torres-Padilla is the Director of the Institute of Epigenetics and Stem Cells at Helmholtz Centre Munich and Professor of Stem Cell Biology at the Faculty of Biology of the Ludwig Maximilian University Munich. Her lab works on understanding the epigenetic principles underlying cellular reprogramming, totipotency and early mammalian development.

other, including H4K20me3 and H3K64me3 in constitutive heterochromatin and H2AK119 ubiquitylation in facultative heterochromatin, whereas DNA methylation, for example, can be found in both. It is generally true that H3K27me3 is cell-type-dependent, marking developmentally regulated genes and various chromosome features such as the inactive X chromosome in mammals. Thus, in general, the presence of H3K27me3 and the Polycomb machinery together is an appropriate indicator of facultative heterochromatin. By contrast, the classical marker of constitutive heterochromatin, H3K9me3, is also located in non-centromeric or non-telomeric regions, spanning both genes and interspersed repeats in a cell-type-dependent manner as highlighted by recent work both in mammalian cells and in Caenorhabditis elegans2,3. Thus, the use of H3K9me3 to exclusively define constitutive heterochromatin is invalid and instead, a combination of the above-mentioned molecular descriptions should be utilized. As with many molecular studies, the devil lies in the details: the more molecular information and layers of regulation we discover, the more exceptions to the rule arise. As an example from an evolutionary standpoint, in early land plants, Polycomb is associated with constitutive heterochromatin, including transposons, whereas in Schizosaccharomyces pombe and mammals, equivalent regions such as satellites are marked by H3K9me3-driven heterochromatin. In fact, H3K27me3 has been recently proposed to be the ancestral molecular marker of constitutive heterochromatin. In general, we propose that a genetic definition may better distinguish between facultative and constitutive heterochromatin across eukaryotes, whereby transposable elements and structural

[Page 3]

genomic features such as centromeres and telomeres are more consistently fitting with the notion of constitutive heterochromatin. O.B. I think the subdivision into two types of heterochromatin is still appropriate. Constitutive heterochromatin annotates telomeres, pericentromeric regions and other chromosomal domains, which are invariably transcriptionally repressed in all cell types. These genomic regions are almost always marked by H3K9me2 or H3K9me3. By contrast, facultative heterochromatin is dynamically regulated and capable of interconverting to euchromatin in response to physiological stimuli such as developmental cues. A prominent example of facultative heterochromatin is the cell-type-specific silencing of developmental genes by PRC1 and PRC2. H3K27me3 is catalysed by PRC2 and recognized by PRC1, which in turn affects chromatin folding and drives transcription repression. H3K27me3 also serves as an epigenetic signal promoting heterochromatin spreading in cis and inheritance of repressive chromatin domains through genome replication. However, regulation of facultative heterochromatin is not limited to H3K27me3-dependent mechanisms. Recent studies revealed differential silencing by variant PRC1 complexes, independent of H3K27me3. Additionally, H3K9me2-dependent and H3K9me3dependent repression of transposon activity, commonly associated with constitutive heterochromatin, is frequently co-opted for differential regulation of cell-type-specific gene expression. Therefore, different chromatin modifiers and combinations of histone PTMs can contribute to facultative heterochromatin. It remains to be seen whether all these repressive pathways share mechanistic features of sequence-independent spreading and epigenetic inheritance. Do you think there has been a paradigm shift regarding maintenance of hetero chromatin features and functions (during development, ageing and between generations) in the past 20 years? If so, why and what shift? C.D. Our understanding of mechanisms involved in heterochromatin (epigenetic) inheritance has certainly changed. A considerable paradigm shift is occurring in recognizing a requirement for re-nucleation — in addition to chromatin modifier read–write mechanisms — for epigenetic inheritance in dividing cells. This requirement is well characterized in

S. pombe and involves transient pulses of transcription at a specific phase of the cell cycle within the H3K9me2 centromeric repeats. In Polycomb-mediated silencing, re-nucleation of Polycomb proteins in cis at specific sites (defined as Polycomb response elements in fruitflies) is required for long-term silencing in addition to H3K27me3 read–write feedbacks4,5. Furthermore, it is clear that hetero chromatin features can change with ageing, which has given rise to the topical concept of the epigenetic clock in mammalian cells. Thinking has also shifted with respect to intergenerational inheritance of heterochromatin. There was a flurry of studies several years ago looking at whether environmentally induced epigenetic changes in one generation could be passed between generations through heterochromatin. To date, intergenerational inheritance in mammals seems to be mediated by functions of regulatory (small) RNAs, as there is effective resetting of DNA methylation states in the gametes and embryo. It is different in plants in that stable DNA methylation states can contribute to intergenerational inheritance, but this too is likely to involve siRNA-mediated inheritance in the gametes and embryos. S.G. One might indeed say that the definition of heterochromatin has been refined in recent years, given that the functional definition of heterochromatin and many of its properties were initially based on the phenomena of position effect variegation in flies or telomeric position effect in yeasts. Both of these involve the variegated repression of a euchromatin gene following its unnatural insertion or translocation near a site of constitutive hetero chromatin (for example, near centromeric or telomeric repeats)6. The term heterochromatin now includes stably repressed chromatin states that are relevant for development and cell-type determination. Position effect variegation and telomeric position effect are both characterized by a certain stochasticity, linear spreading along the chromosome and mitotic heritability, once a heterochromatin domain had been established. The recently discovered role of heterochromatin in cell-type determination — encompassing both Polycomb-repressed genes held in a poised state and H3K9me3-marked genes repressed in a tissue-specific manner — renders heterochromatin much more interesting, because it shows that cell-type integrity is defined not only by the set of genes that are active, but also by the stable repression of others. Whereas differentiation into nearly all somatic cell types can occur in C. elegans in the absence of H3K9 methylation, the stable

maintenance of differentiated tissues is compromised in its absence, allowing tissues to assume premature-ageing phenotypes3. There are two other ways in which the definition of heterochromatin has changed: originally it was defined as a domain, often nucleated by a repetitive element, that can spread along the chromatin fibre and aberrantly repress nearby genes. This process often involves H3K9 methylation. Now we know that a domain of heterochromatin can exactly cover a gene (including the promoter) and appears to be nucleated by a specific subset of transcription factors. To reverse the repression mediated by a gene-associated domain of heterochromatin, it is not sufficient to lose H3K9 methylation: the active recruitment of histone acetyltransferases, which is also triggered by select transcription factors, is needed to generate a stable ‘open’ or transcribed chromatin state. This reversal can occur in a programmed manner during tissue differentiation, and often is associated with a physical shift of an inactive gene from the nuclear periphery to the open chromatin of the nuclear interior. A final change in our understanding of heterochromatin has to do with its inheritance through cell division. Early definitions of epigenetic inheritance focused on the transmission of repressed states between generations. Our current concept of heterochromatin, particularly in the context of cell-type-specific or facultative heterochromatin, stresses its mitotic inheritance — that is, its persistence through mitotic cell division. Heterochromatin does not have to be re-nucleated by transcription factor–histone methyltransferase interaction or by reader–writer interaction in each cell cycle, although without re-nucleation heterochromatin will eventually be lost. M.E.T.P. & A.B. Although we have learned much about heterochromatin over the past 20 years, we highlight three discoveries that we consider as conceptual shifts, for the reasons we discuss below. The first of these is the dynamicity of both facultative and constitutive heterochromatin composition across developmental time and cell types. The second is the realization that heterochromatin is not necessarily transcriptionally inert. The third is the challenge to the conception of heterochromatin as a dominant repressive system, considering that absence of H3K9me3 does not necessarily lead to transcription activation. One of the major advances in the field of chromatin biology in the past 20 years has been the widespread mapping of the genomic localization of many chromatin-associated

factors across tissues and species by ChIP-seq and associated techniques. We have learned of the considerable repetitive and non-coding make-up of our genomes and the vast complexity in the distribution of chromatin-associated factors and PTMs. Although structural regions of the genome such as centromeres and telomeres tend to be marked in a highly conserved manner by features of constitutive heterochromatin, other regions of the genome, including interspersed repetitive elements, differ substantially in their chromatin composition between cell types. Thus, there is a considerable dynamicity in the arrangement of both facultative and constitutive heterochromatin, particularly during development of multicellular organisms. During the past 20 years it has also become clear that heterochromatin regions are not as stable and inert as originally thought. For example, transcriptional activity in constitutive heterochromatin regions such as centromeric sequences is observed in multiple model organisms — from yeast to early mammalian embryos — and in disease. In fact, transcription is essential for heterochromatin assembly and nucleation, and in some cases for its spreading too, as exemplified by transcription of the non-coding RNA X-inactive-specific transcript, which is crucial for establishing X chromosome inactivation in eutherians. In addition to transcriptional activity, 20 years ago a prominent role was discovered for RNAi in heterochromatin assembly at centromeres and silent mating-type loci, which suggested that heterochromatin was more dynamic than previously believed. Recent work by several labs and in different model organisms indicates that H3K9me3 is not necessarily transcriptionally repressive. This realization of course opens up very exciting research opportunities, which should address what the actual mechanisms are that lead to transcription repression. Furthermore, it has recently been shown that absence of H3K9me3 — for example, during cell reprogramming or development — is not sufficient to derepress heterochromatin. Instead, a functional coordination of specific transcription factors and the heterochromatin machinery is fine-tuned to regulate developmental or differentiation-specific repression3. We believe that we still do not quite fully understand how this process is regulated, although of course cell-type-specific expression of transcription factors is key for this regulation. Thus, a certain ‘ambivalence’ of the functional properties of heterochromatin has emerged over the last years, mainly related

to its transcriptional capacity. Sure enough, such capacity is essential for the three main molecular features of heterochromatin — nucleation, spreading and maintenance — that we described above. The integration of the different mechanisms that generate the hetero chromatic make-up across cell types and evolution, but also their relevance for human disease, will surely continue to keep us busy and excited for many years to come.

three-dimensional genome organization. In fact, several heterochromatin effectors, which are required for inheritance, support chromosome folding and/or possibly liquid– liquid phase separation. Resolving how these 3D structures contribute to heterochromatin dynamics presents an exciting challenge for the field and will have important implications for understanding the mechanism of epigenetic inheritance.

O.B. I think that the paradigm that was established 20 years ago is still largely valid today, but it has been refined. The original paradigm proposes that coupling between readers and writers of histone PTMs reinforces heritable maintenance of heterochromatin7. Since then, scientists have identified many PTM readers and writers, and novel technologies to study epigenetics have provided strong evidence that positive feedback loops between these activities promote inheritance of heterochromatin. For example, the Moazed and Allshire labs showed that artificial tethering of a writer of H3K9me2 and H3K9me3 was sufficient to establish heterochromatin and transcriptional gene silencing at an engineered reporter locus in fission yeast. Notably, the ectopic heterochromatin domain persisted for many cell divisions after releasing the tethered writer from the nucleation site, demonstrating that these histone PTMs can mediate sequence-independent maintenance of heterochromatin through cell division8,9. The silent heterochromatin could even be maintained through meiosis, suggesting that the PTMs even serve as carriers of epigenetic information for transgenerational inheritance of repressive chromatin. In addition to underpinning the original paradigm, recent advances also highlighted that stability of repressive domains is governed by a dynamic interplay between heterochromatinmodifying and euchromatin-modifying activities. For example, inheritance of the artificially induced heterochromatin described above was antagonized by anti-silencing factors such as the H3K9-specific demethylase Epe1 (also known as Jhd1). Balancing the activities of opposing chromatin modifiers enables spatiotemporal control of heterochromatin maintenance, which is crucial to avoiding erroneous and potentially deleterious gene silencing during development3. Consistently, aberrant gain or loss of heterochromatin resulting from an imbalance in opposing modifier activities has been linked to ageing and pathogenesis of human diseases, including cancer7. Finally, beyond PTM-mediated regulation, heterochromatin stability is influenced by
